imageCaption: The Córdoba central plaza at dusk, with rippling patterns of light overlaying the ancient stones—like watching frequency waves become visible, or like watching the ground remember how to vibrate.
text:
The central plaza in Córdoba was a perfect circle, which Aaliyah was increasingly learning meant it had been deliberately constructed with temporal mathematics in mind, whether the architects knew what they were doing or not. History was full of people who'd built things in exactly the right places without understanding why those places were right. They'd just felt it.

Like Anthony booking flights without a plan.

Like Luisana deciding this impossible family was worth keeping.

Like Elijah understanding what Sully's barks meant before he had language for it.

The plaza was full of people doing normal evening things—sitting at cafés, taking photographs, the kind of human behavior that usually irritated Aaliyah because everyone was too focused on documenting where they were instead of actually being there. But something was different here. The tourists and locals weren't moving quite in sync. They were moving like a system with multiple internal rhythms—some people walking faster, some slower, and underneath it all, a bass-note quality to the evening that suggested the plaza itself was breathing.

"Sully's doing the thing," Elijah reported. He was gripping the dog's collar with the focused attention of someone who knew major glitches required supervision. "The one where he vibrates before he barks."

Aaliyah felt it too. Not through Sully. Through the stones beneath her feet. The plaza was humming—a sound so low it was more vibration than audio, the kind of frequency that lived just below the threshold of hearing but absolutely above the threshold of feeling. She'd read once that elephants communicated through subsonic frequencies. That whales sent messages across entire oceans using sound waves that no human ear could consciously process but every human nervous system could register as wrongness.

This plaza was speaking.

"We should move to the center," Aaliyah said. She was doing the thing she did when her brain had outpaced her emotional readiness—talking fast, moving fast, trying to think through problems before feelings could catch up. "If there's a measurement point, it'll be at the exact center. That's where the frequency will be strongest."

Her father was already moving, of course. Anthony approached most situations like they were puzzles waiting to be solved with enthusiasm and a good attitude. Her mother was writing, documenting the way the plaza seemed to be adjusting its geometry as they walked—the café tables shifting incrementally, the fountain water flowing in directions that didn't quite match gravity's expectations, the light changing color in ways that suggested the plaza was cycling through emotional states.

The center of the plaza was marked by a stone fountain. Not ornate, not particularly impressive from any historical standpoint, just an old fountain doing the very specific work of being an anchor point for broken time. Water flowed from it in multiple directions simultaneously—some falling down, some falling up, some flowing sideways into the stone itself like the fountain was teaching the ground how to dream.

"Everyone hold hands," Luisana said. It wasn't a question. She'd extended her hand to Aaliyah on one side and to Anthony on the other, and the instruction carried the weight of someone who'd understood months ago that they operated as a unified system now, that their frequencies were most powerful when they were literally connected, touching, reinforcing each other's resonance.

Aaliyah took her mother's hand. Anthony took her father's, then reached for Elijah, who was still gripping Sully's collar with the focus of a boy and his dog conducting an important science experiment. The formation wasn't a circle—it was something more complex, five points of connection (counting Sully, whose frequency apparently counted and whose heartbeat was definitely audible now, not just to Elijah but to everyone) creating a resonance pattern that made the plaza stones glow.

Not light. Glow. As though the rock itself was remembering that it was made of ancient sunlight, compressed through millions of years into something solid, and now it was being asked to remember that frequency.

"What's happening?" Elijah whispered.

"We're measuring ourselves," Aaliyah said. She could feel it now—her heartbeat, her mother's heartbeat, her father's distinct rhythm (which was somehow both calmer and faster than normal, a paradox her heart understood even if her brain was struggling), Elijah's quick excited pulse, and underneath it all, Sully's deep steady drumbeat, consistent as gravity.

The fountain water began to flow in a pattern that looked almost like a waveform. Up and down, crest and trough, the visual representation of the sound the plaza was making. Aaliyah's phone buzzed again—ARIA-7 clearly had no respect for the boundaries of technology—and she didn't even need to look at it to know what the message said. But she looked anyway, because information was a form of control and she needed to feel like she was controlling something.

*Resonance frequency optimized. Your family unit has achieved harmonic alignment with 94% efficiency. Previous measurement: 87%. Improvement rate: 3.5% per day. At current acceleration rate, you will achieve perfect synchronization on Day 61. This is projected as the moment of greatest stability and greatest danger. Your frequency will be so powerful it will collapse all remaining temporal fractures into a single coherent point. Whether that point is a reset, a solution, or a catastrophe depends on your collective intention. Choose wisely. You have 15 days to increase from 94% to 100% resonance.*

She didn't share the message immediately. She stood there in the plaza while her family's combined heartbeat made the fountain do impossible things, and she understood with the kind of clarity that comes from being seventeen and terrified that the problem had changed.

They weren't just trying to stabilize the glitches anymore.

They were trying to become perfect enough that their heartbeat could remake reality in their image.

"Lulu," Anthony said quietly, squeezing his wife's hand. "I'm scared."

"I know," Luisana said. "So am I."

The morpho butterfly appeared above the fountain, its wings catching the light of the glowing plaza stones, turning blue into something that looked like peace. Abuela Ida, checking in. Abuela Ida, approving.

Abuela Ida, who'd always told Anthony that the best things in life were unplanned, standing witness to what happened when unplanned became inevitable.

"We should keep going," Elijah said, because he was seven and understood fear but mostly just wanted to know what happened next. "Sully says there's more. Something bigger. Something that needs all of us beating at the same time."

Aaliyah looked at her family, their hands connected, their frequencies merged into something that was no longer quite individual. She thought about her phone message. About fifteen days. About the moment when their love for each other would become so perfectly synchronized that it could either save the world or transform it into something they didn't recognize anymore.

She squeezed her mother's hand.

"Okay," she said. "Let's figure out how to be perfect."

The plaza sang beneath their feet, a frequency that sounded like family, like heartbeat, like coming home to people you'd never left.

Like time itself learning how to love the chaos it contained.
